coredata.dc:title: Single-layered graphene quantum dots with self-passivated layer from xylan for visual detection of trace chromium(Vl)
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Jihai
Han, Guangda
Ren, Junli
Liu, Chuanfu
Wang, Jianlong
Wang, Xiaoying
coredata.dc:description: Single-layered graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are commonly prepared through bottom-up strategy by aromatic molecules or other carbon precursors with complicated procedure. Herein, single-layered GQDs were firstly fabricated by nonaromatic xylan only with the assistance of NaOH/urea under hydrothermal conditions. Due to the gases (ammonia and carbon oxide) released by decomposition of urea, GQDs were single-layered and hindered from interaction and aggregation for the protection of self-passivated layer. The synthesized GQDs showed a fluorescent quantum yield of 23.8%, self-passivated xylan layer on the surface of GQDs avoided ions-induced interference, and were only destroyed by strong oxidants like those containing Cr(VI). And there were the inner filter effect and static quenching for chelating interaction between Cr(VI) and nitrogen, oxygen functional group from GODs. These results entrusted GQDs with good selectivity and sensitivity toward Cr(VI). Furthermore, in order to realize on-site visual detection of Cr(VI), a microfluidic chip and a smartphone were used to construct portable detection platform. The linear detection range was from 3 μM to 75 μM and the limit of detection was 0.10 μM, which was lower than that of GQDs solution through fluorescence spectrophotometer. Therefore, this equipment provides a simple method to on-site monitor water environment.

head.ce:title: Single-layered graphene quantum dots with self-passivated layer from xylan for visual detection of trace chromium(Vl)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Single-layered graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are commonly prepared through bottom-up strategy by aromatic molecules or other carbon precursors with complicated procedure. Herein, single-layered GQDs were firstly fabricated by nonaromatic xylan only with the assistance of NaOH/urea under hydrothermal conditions. Due to the gases (ammonia and carbon oxide) released by decomposition of urea, GQDs were single-layered and hindered from interaction and aggregation for the protection of self-passivated layer. The synthesized GQDs showed a fluorescent quantum yield of 23.8%, self-passivated xylan layer on the surface of GQDs avoided ions-induced interference, and were only destroyed by strong oxidants like those containing Cr(VI). And there were the inner filter effect and static quenching for chelating interaction between Cr(VI) and nitrogen, oxygen functional group from GODs. These results entrusted GQDs with good selectivity and sensitivity toward Cr(VI). Furthermore, in order to realize on-site visual detection of Cr(VI), a microfluidic chip and a smartphone were used to construct portable detection platform. The linear detection range was from 3 μM to 75 μM and the limit of detection was 0.10 μM, which was lower than that of GQDs solution through fluorescence spectrophotometer. Therefore, this equipment provides a simple method to on-site monitor water environment.

ce:section.ce:para: As a burgeoning type of fluorescence (FL) nanomaterials with high quantum yield (QY), photobleaching resistance, biocompatibility, and tunable band gap, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are widely applied in pigment, lighting, photovoltaics, photocatalysis, sensing, and bioimaging. In order to satisfy demands in special applications, their photoluminescence (PL) properties need to be tailored by various parameters, such as the size of sp2 clusters, defects, heteroatom doping, edge configuration, and functional groups [1]. However, for multi-layered GQDs with large heterogeneity, applying one tuning strategy sometimes leads to contradictory experimental observation [2]. Therefore, single-layered GQDs with an exact chemical structure and number of composed atoms are beneficial for special applications.

To date, there are two categories of methods to synthesize single-layered GQDs. In top-down methods, graphite or coal is broken into GQDs in a less eco-friendly route by chemical oxidation with nitric acid and sulfuric acid [3–5]. Other approaches like plasma etching [6], laser ablation [7], intercalation-exfoliation [8] have also been reported, but they require elaborate preparation of carbon nanomaterials (such as single-layered graphene, carbon nano-onion, carbon quantum dots), which blocks the mass production. Due to variously optional carbon precursors, the bottom-up approaches have advantages over these top-down methods in heteroatom doping and composition tuning. Wang et al. fabricated single-layered GQDs by molecular fusion of perylene tetracarboxylic anhydride with polyethyleneimine under solvothermal conditions [9]. The aromatic structure of perylene tetracarboxylic anhydride was considered as nanoscale fragment of graphene, which contributed to the formation of graphene sheet. Liu et al. synthesized single-layered GQDs through pyrolysis of citric acid in the restricted nanospace of layered double hydroxides (LDHs) [10]. The citric acid was intercalated into the layer gallery of LDHs by co-precipitation. After carbonization, LDHs were removed by hydrochloric acid. In general, single-layered GQDs prepared by bottom-up routes are derived from either aromatic molecules, or other molecules which require complicated procedure, so seeking a facile strategy to transform nonaromatic molecule to single-layer GQDs is strongly desired.

Xylan is a kind of nonaromatic molecule with backbone chain of β-(1 → 4) linked D-xylose, which is the main component of hemicelluloses. Its low cost, renewability and abundant resource make xylan a promising carbon precursor. And compared with cellulose and lignin, xylan is more thermal-chemically sensitive [11]. It is reported that the solvothermal treatment of xylan in choline chloride/urea [12] or aqueous ammonia [13,14] could induce the generation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). However, there is no research on the fabrication of single-layered GQDs from xylan. Inspired by delamination of CQDs into single-layered GQDs through dissolving and evaporating methanol [8], we assumed that if the carbonization of xylan was accompanied with expanding gas, single-layered GQDs were most likely to be realized in a bottom-up approach. NaOH/urea system, which is a good solvent for xylan, has the ability to generate ammonia and carbon dioxide gas under hydrothermal conditions [15]. Furthermore, the ion size and free volume in the ionic systems of NaOH/urea are relatively smaller than that of choline chloride/urea, and hence NaOH/urea may form more stable and adequate complex with xylan than aqueous ammonia [16]. All those advantages of NaOH/urea may lead to the formation of single-layered GQDs through the solvothermal treatment of xylan.

Herein, xylan was chosen as carbon precursor. By NaOH/urea, xylan was fully dissolved in a way of forming a complex compound with NaOH, urea and H2O [17,18]. During the carbonization process, the urea complexed with xylan decomposed into ammonia and carbon oxide. The release of ammonia and carbon oxide around xylan avoided the interaction and aggregation, leading to the successful fabrication of N-doped single-layered GQDs. Compared with naked GQDs, the prepared single-layered GQDs are surface-passivated with incompletely carbonized xylan, which shields their fluorescence (FL) against the interference of ions in water. Besides, only strong oxidizing agents (like KCr2O7 and KMnO4) can damage the passivated layer of the GQDs, causing selective quenching of FL. Furthermore, due to the rich nitrogen and oxygen groups, GQDs can form a stable ground state by chelating with Cr(VI) [19], leading to effective static quenching and enhanced inner filter effect. All these features show that the fabricated GQDs is a promising FL probe for the selective detection of Cr(VI).

Cr(VI) is widely used in the manufacture of pigments and paints, leather, catalysts, fungicides, which would be discharged into aquatic environment [20]. As a kind of heavy metal chemical element, Cr(VI) with high oxidation potential has intense biotoxicity and leads to sever disease such as cancer, renal failure, and embryo malformation et al. with its accumulation. Even in low concentration, Cr(VI) is much toxic and particularly poisonous [21]. To ensure the security of drinking and agricultural water, it is necessary to achieve on-site and real-time monitoring of Cr(VI). Metal-organic frameworks (MOF), such as Cd-MOFs, Cu-MOFs, Eu-MOFs, Ag-MOFs, Zn-MOFs, Zr-MOFs, have been extensive fabricated for their modifiable structures and outstanding optical properties. Although their limit of detection (LOD) could be low to nanomoles per liter, they were simultaneously sensitive to Cr(VI) and other contaminants [22–24], which hindered the determination of Cr(VI) in real polluted water. Other materials like metal clusters [25], hydroxide [26,27], and fluorescent polymers [28] were developed for sensing Cr(VI), but their synthesis procedures were complex and their fluorescence properties were hard to regulate. Besides them, fluorescence quantum dots were another promising kind of probe. Their detection capabilities could be improved by element doping element doping (N [29], P [30], or S [31], et al.), surface functionalization [32], scaffolds loading [33]. However, these detection methods were laborious and carried out in laboratory with complex experiment and analysis processes.

Therefore, we integrated the GQDs into a portable microfluidic device and constructed an intelligent detection platform based on smartphone to achieve on-site and real-time detection (Fig. 1 ). The GQDs were embedded in a cellulose nanofiber (CNF) strengthened polyacrylamide (PAAm) hydrogel to indicate Cr(VI). Through the FL image processing function of smartphone, the concentration of Cr(VI) is determined according to the captured image of the hydrogel under ultraviolet illumination, and thus a low-cost, rapid and visual detection method with the ability to realize point-of-care test is established.

Xylan was extracted from bagasse. The extracted method of xylan and its chemical formula were shown in supplementary material (Note S1 and Fig. S1). Potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7, AR) was obtained from Guangdong Guanghua Technilogy Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China).

1 g of xylan was dissolved in 20 mL of NaOH/urea (8%/12%, w/w) aqueous solution. The mixture was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in a muffle furnace (ELF11/6B/301, purchased from Verder Shanghai Instruments and Equipment Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China)) at 240 °C for 24 h. The obtained mixture solution was centrifuged at 14000 rpm for 10 min to remove precipitate. After dialysis for a week with dialysis bag of 500 Da molecular weight cut off (MWCO), single-layered GQDs were prepared and stored at room temperature away from light.

Under stirring, 3 g of acrylamide, oxidized nanocellulose, 0.03 g of ammonium persulfate and 0.01 g of N, N'-methylene bisacrylamide were added respectively to 3 mL of 0.25 mg/mL single-layered GQDs aqueous dispersion. Then, 20 μL of tetramethyl ethylenediamine was added, and 20 μL of the mixture was poured in “Y” shaped mold. After cross-linking at 40 °C for 20 min, the GQDs/PAAm hydrogel was peeled from the mold.

The UV–vis spectrum was investigated by a UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Daojin, China). FT-IR spectra were investigated on a Tensor 27 infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). TEM images were taken by a JEM-2010F transmittance electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). AFM images were obtained by a Nano Scope III atomic force microscope (Veeco Metrology Group, America). XPS spectra were measured by an ESCALAB 250 (Themo-VG Scientific, America). The obtained XPS peaks were deconvoluted by XPSpeak41. SEM images were obtained by a merlin field emission scanning electron microscope (Zeiss, Germany). The mechanical properties were measured by a tensile tester 3000 (Instron, America).

The FL spectra of GQDs were measure on a fluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba, America). The FL spectra of GQDs were measure on a fluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba, America). The quenching efficiency (QE) of Cr(VI) toward GQDs was determined by following equation [34]:
(1)
QE = 1 -
F /
F 0
where F0 was the FL intensity of GQDs and F was the FL intensity of GQDs with 150 μM Cr(VI). The value of quenching efficiency was applied to describe the decrease of FL caused by ions.

The layout of the microfluidic chip is presented in Fig. 1. It is a sandwich structure composed of organic glass-polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)-organic glass. The overall dimension of the chip is 2.9 cm × 2.3 cm. The thickness of organic glass layer and the PDMS layer is 3 mm and 1 mm, respectively. To fabricating the PDMS layer, a master mold was prepared by directly writing microchannel geometry on the substrate of organic glass with a laser carving system (provided by Shandong Juming Laser Equipment Co., Ltd. (Liaocheng, China)). Silicone elastomer and curing agent were mixed at a 10:1 wt ratio and were poured on the master mold, followed by degassing and curing at 70 °C for 6 h. Finally, the PDMS layer was peeled from the master mold. To assemble each layer, six holes were punched on the edge of organic glass, and the three layers were fastened by screws.

Conventional fluorescence analysis was carried out by measuring the fluorescence intensity of probe on a spectrophotometer. Nowadays, smart phone is developed to be a powerful integrated equipment. The high-definition camera loaded on smart phone was qualified for capturing the fluorescence image under the irradiation of a UV lamp. And with the aid of image processing apps, the brightness of the image was digitized as grayscale, which was equal to fluorescence intensity. Therefore, by analyzing the change of brightness after addition of Cr(VI), the concentration of Cr(VI) could be determined. The detailed procedures were showed as below:

The prepared GQDs/PAAm hydrogel with thickness of 1 mm was integrated with the microfluidic chip as a FL probe. Then, K2Cr2O7 solution in pH = 7.4 was injected into the microfluidic chip at 1 cm3/min by a LSP02-2A syringe pump (Longer, China). Under the radiation of a 365 nm UV beam (the distance between lamp and chip was 8 cm), the picture of GQDs/PAAm hydrogel was taken by a smart phone with 40 megapixel in dark environment. The FL intensity was estimated by pasting the picture to Adobe Photoshop CC 2014 in color mode of grayscale. The average grayscale (G) of the hydrogel in the picture was calculated by the software to represent the FL intensity/brightness. By analyzing the decrease of brightness, the concentration of Cr(VI) was determined.

Lake water was used as real polluted water samples. Measured by ICP-OES, the element concentrations in lake water like Cr, Al, Na, Ca, Ba, Fe, Cu, Mg, Mn, Cd, Pb, Ni, Zn, K, Hg, and Ag were 0.000273, <0.005, 38.8, 32.9, 0.0256, 0.00446, 0.000752, 3.26, 0.00006, <0.0005, <0.0001, 0.00285, 0.00341, 13.8, <0.0005, and < 0.002 mg/L, respectively.

The effect of reaction temperature and time on the formation and performance of GQDs were investigated. As shown in Table 1 , the temperature had a major influence on the thickness of GQDs. In the narrow temperature range from 180 °C to 240 °C, the thickness of GQDs was decreased sharply with the increase of temperature. It may be due to the high temperature accelerated the hydrolysis rate and degree of xylan, thus, without the intertwining of xylan molecular chain, GQDs avoided aggregation and dispersed evenly in aqueous solution (Fig. S2a-S2c, Fig. 3b). Meanwhile, for the enhanced carbonization of GQDs by increased temperature and elimination of aggregation-caused quenching, the QY of GQDs was improved about 54 times from 0.43% to 23.8%, which showed promising potential as fluorescence probe. For the sake that the highest working temperature of Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave was 240 °C, the reaction temperature cannot exceed 240 °C. The reaction time has similar effect with temperature on GQDs, and the reaction exceed 24 h leaded to the aggregation and decreased QY of GQDs. Since the GQDs-4 prepared at 240 °C for 24 h had the highest QY and lowest thickness, it was selected for the further study.

As illustrated in Fig. 2
, a possible growth mechanism is proposed for the preparation of single-layered GQDs. Xylan is a branched heteropolysaccharide with xylose, arabinose, and glucuronic acid units. In the NaOH/urea system, NaOH hydrates attach to xylan chain through hydrogen bond and urea hydrates wrap NaOH hydrogen-bonded xylan, leading to formation of channel inclusion compound (Fig. 2a) [35]. During the hydrothermal process, xylan is hydrolyzed and carbonized, meanwhile urea is decomposed, with the generation of NH3 and CO2 gas. Exfoliated by the NH3 and CO2 gas, the prepared GQDs tend to be monolayer. Furthermore, the nitrogen-containing radicals are incorporated into GQDs, leading to the doping of nitrogen element. And incompletely carbonized xylan remains as C–H group on the edge of GQDs, which passivates the surface of GQDs [36].

In the hydrothermal reaction of xylan, NaOH/urea was chosen as a better solvent to dissolve xylan compared to choline chloride/urea and aqueous ammonia, meanwhile nitrogen doping was achieved, which promoted the FL performance of GQDs. The content of N doped in GQDs and its chemical state were measured by XPS. As shown in the full scan XPS spectra (Fig. 2b), three typical peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.8 eV, and 530.8 eV are assigned to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. And the atomic concentration of O, N and C are 19.28%, 1.38% and 79.34%, respectively. The existence of N1s confirms the anchor of N atoms to carbon skeleton of GQDs in hydrothermal treatment. In C1s high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 2c), there are three kinds of carbon: nitrous carbon, oxygenated carbon, and graphitic carbon (C–C and C = C). The peaks of specific bonding type like C = C, C–C, C-N, C-O and C = O/C = N are centered at 284.3 eV, 284.8 eV, 285.6 eV, 286.4 eV and 288.8 eV, respectively [37]. The ratio of sp3 to sp2 bonded carbon atoms is calculated as 1.55, which is due to lots of oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on the surface of GQDs. This ration was further investigated by Raman spectrum. As shown in Fig. S3, there are two main modes including D mode and G mode. D mode originates from the defects and edges of GQDs (sp3 bonded carbon atoms) and G mode comes from the sp2 carbon domains. The ID/IG is 1.35, consistent with the ratio of sp3 to sp2 bonded carbon atoms calculated by of XPS spectrum. The bonding between carbon and oxygen in GQDs is further analyzed in O1s high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 2d). The peaks at 530.5 eV, 531.3 eV and 532.6 eV are originated from C = O, O-C = O/N-C = O and C-O-C/C-OH, respectively [38]. In N1s high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 2e), the peaks at 399.6 eV, 400.7 eV, 401.8 eV, 402.4 eV and 406.2 eV are attributed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N, oxidized N [39] and molecular N, respectively. The signal of molecular N was originated from N2 gas in air, which was adsorbed on the GQDs for their large surface area. Among speciation of doped-nitrogen, pyrrolic N would enhance the FL of GQDs [40]. Since pyrrolic N is the major nitrogen in GQDs which takes up 44.4%, a high FL performance is guaranteed. All these results reveal that the xylan derived GQDs are consisted of sp2 carbon cluster with sp3 carbon defects and oxygen–nitrogen-rich edges.

The micrograph of GQDs was measured by TEM and AFM to investigate their single-layered structure. As shown in Fig. 3a, GQDs are monodispersed quasi-sphere and their size is about 3.2 ± 0.2 nm. In the inset of Fig. 3a, there is a crystal lattice of 0.24 nm attributed to (1120) crystal phase of graphite [41]. This confirms a certain crystallinity of GQDs. The AFM image of GQDs (Fig. 3b) shows their height is mainly distributed in a narrow range of 0.3–0.5 nm, revealing that the GQDs are mainly composed of single graphite layer and would have an outstanding quantum confinement effect [42]. Therefore, it is confirmed that the designed strategy is an efficient and feasible way to prepare single-layered GQDs.

The FT-IR spectra of xylan and GQDs are shown in Fig. 3c, the typical absorption band of xylan at 3406 cm−1, 2913 cm−1, 1653 cm−1 are ascribed to bending vibration of O–H, stretching vibration of C–H, C = O, respectively. Furthermore, the characteristic absorption band of xylan at 1036 cm−1 is assigned to C-O-C in glycosidic bond and C–C stretching vibration. The characteristic absorption band of xylan at 895 cm−1 is originated from β-D-glycoside bond between sugar units [43]. Other two small bands at 1333 cm−1 and 1249 cm−1 are belonged to C-O stretching in carboxylic acid or carboxylate [44], and C-O in acetyl group [45], respectively.

Compared with the FT-IR spectra of xylan, the characteristic absorption band of GQDs around 1653 cm−1 and 1333 cm−1 are strengthened, indicating the oxidation of aldehyde group in hydrothermal reaction [46]. Meanwhile, due to the hydrolysis of glycosidic bond and the breaking of C–C bonds, the intensity of peak at 1036 cm−1 decreases and the peak at 895 cm−1 even disappears. Furthermore, for the hydrolysis of acetyl group, the peak at 1249 cm−1 disappears. Although, these results show that xylan pyrolyzed in hydrothermal reactions, a weaker absorption of C–H vibration at 2923 cm−1 still exists, indicating that there is incompletely carbonized xylan in GQDs [36]. New peaks at 1923 cm−1, 835 cm−1, and 696 cm−1 arise, which are corresponded to -C = C = C-, C–H in benzene(para-substituted)/pyridine/pyrimidine ring, and C–H in benzene ring, respectively. The result reveals the form of graphene honeycomb lattice [47]. Furthermore, due to the adsorption of CO2 on the surface of GQDs, a peak around 2546 cm−1 corresponded to O = C = O emerges, indicating the exfoliation to form single-layered GQDs by CO2.

The prepared GQDs showed excellent solubility and stability in water and emit bright blue FL under the illuminate of a 365 nm UV-beam (the inset of Fig. 3d). Its blue FL is originated from sp2 structure by transition between lowest unoccupied molecular orbital and highest occupied molecular orbital [48]. In the UV–vis adsorption spectra of GQDs, the absorption peaks at 222 nm and 271 nm are attributed to the π-π* transition of C = C bonds, while the shoulder peak at 334 nm is originated from the n-π* transition of C = O/C = N bonds [49]. The maximum FL emission is observed at 434 nm, and its excitation wavelength is located at 350 nm. The excitation dependent photoluminescence property of GQDs is shown in Fig. 3e. With the increase of excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 370 nm, the emission gradually shifts from 424 nm to 442 nm, which is similar to the FL behavior of GQDs in literature [50–52]. Selecting quinine sulfate as the standard, the GQDs was measured to have a FL QY of 23.8%. As shown in Table 2 , compared with QY of single-layered GQDs prepared by other methods, the QY of GQDs synthesized with the assistance of NaOH in this work is relatively high. Investigated by time correlated single photon counting method, the time-resolved FL decay curve (Fig. 3f) was obtained. The decay curve can be fitted to a double-exponential function as I(t) = 203(-t/2.67) + 104(-t/8.84), which indicates two discrete states at the bottom of the conduction band [53]. According to the function equation, the average FL lifetime was calculated as 6.55 ns.

As shown in Fig. S4a, the energy gap between conduction band and valance of GQDs was 3.70 eV, which was calculated by Tauc plot. In order to determine the LUMO and HOMO energy levels of GQDs, cyclic voltammetry test was carried out in three-electrode system. The oxidation peak (Fig. S4b) related to electron injections into the valence band was at 1.47 V versus Ag/AgCl. Therefore, EHOMO and ELUMO were calculated as −6.19 eV and −2.49 eV (Fig. S4c), respectively, according to the following three equations [61]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of single-layered GQDs as probe
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the pH value of solution has a great influence on the FL intensity of GQDs, the FL spectrum of GQDs solution with various pH value were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 a, The FL intensity reaches maximum at pH = 7, but it decreases when the solution became acidic or especially became basic. Therefore, the pH value of GQDs solution was adjusted to 7.4 by phosphate buffered saline.

In order to investigate the selectivity of GQDs as a FL probe for the detection of Cr(VI), the responses of GQDs towards various metal ions and anionic ions (such as Cr2O7 2-, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ag+, NO3 –, CO3 –, HCO3 –, Cl-, Ac-, SO4 2-) were measured (Fig. 4b). The quenching efficiency of Cr(VI) toward GQDs can reach 0.8957, which makes the GQDs as highly selective FL probe for Cr(VI). While the quenching efficiency of other ions are below 0.06. The detection of Cr(VI) under the disturbing of various ions were also investigated (Fig. 4c). The presence of other ions can hardly affect FL intensity of GQDs, further indicating good selectivity.

Furthermore, different concentrations of Cr(VI) were involved to investigate the detection sensitivity by GQDs (Fig. 4d). By increasing the concentration of Cr(VI), the FL intensity of GQDs decreases gradually. In the range from 3 μM to 150 μM, the relationship of FL intensity and Cr(VI)’s concentration can be described with a function of log10I = 6.07–0.00779c(Cr(VI)) (μM) (R2 = 0.9975, I is the FL intensity of GQDs) (Fig. 4e). Calculated as 3 times the standard derivation divided by the regression slope (the standard deviation and the slope are derived from Fig. 4e), the limit of detection is 0.43 μM (or equivalently 22 ppm), which is below the guideline in potable water recommended by World Health Organization (50 ppm) [62].

The FL quenching mechanism was investigated to reveal the high quenching efficiency of Cr(VI) towards GQDs. As shown in Fig. 4f, the UV–vis adsorption peak centered at 354 nm is perfectly overlapped with the excitation of GQDs in 300 nm to 400 nm. The light needed for the excitation of the FL of GQDs is absorbed by Cr(VI) in the solution. Furthermore, the FL excitation of GQDs centered at 434 nm can also be absorbed by Cr(VI). Hence, significant quenching of FL was caused by so-called inner filter effect. Although, Fe3+ has the similar spectral overlap with GQDs among virous ions, it has little interference in the detection of Cr(VI) for its tendency to form insoluble oxides in oxygenated environment [63].

Besides the inner filter effect, the static quenching also exists. Compared with naked GQDs, the prepared single-layered GQDs are surface-passivated with incompletely carbonized xylan, which shields their fluorescence (FL) against the interference of ions in water. However, Cr(VI) is a kind of strong oxidant, which can destroy the passivated layer (Fig. 5 a). Furthermore, there are plenty of N and O atoms in GQDs (measured by XPS in Fig. 2), which take up 1.38%, 19.28% of the GQDs, respectively. Therefore, for the notable chemical reactivity of chromium with nitrogen, oxygen (binding energy of Cr-N, Cr-O are 378, 427 KJ/mol, respectively), electron deficient Cr(VI) can bind with nitrogen and oxygen groups on GQDs, which is easier than other interfering ions [19,64,65]. The formation of a stable ground state complex or chelate between GQDs and Cr(VI) changes the electronic structure of GQDs and causes non-irradiative recombination of change carries, thus part of fluorescence is quenched. As shown in Fig. 5b, the quenched FL by inner filter effect is recovered by adding ascorbic acid to reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III). However, with concentration of ascorbic acid increasing from 1350 μM to 1500 μM, the recovered FL intensity remains as 71.8% of GQDs. There is still 28.2% permanent quenched FL, which is raised by static quenching.

This static quenching was confirmed by UV–vis adsorption and FL decay. As shown in Fig. 5c, after the addition of GQDs, the UV–vis adsorption of Cr(VI) becomes stronger and red-shifted, which indicates the formation of complex between GQDs and Cr(VI). The complex would precipitate after standing for a week (the inset of Fig. 5c). Meanwhile, compared with GQDs, the fluorescence life time of GQDs with Cr(VI) (calculated as 6.56 ns) remains unchanged (Fig. 3f).

The destruction of self-passivated layer by Cr(VI) was indicated by FT-IR spectrum. As shown in Fig. 5d, compared with GQDs, the peak corresponded to C–H in benzene(para-substituted)/pyridine/pyrimidine ring at 835 cm−1 becomes weak after the addition of Cr(VI). The decrease of the peak is caused by the destroyed of sp2 domain core after the oxidation of passivated layer, which is related to the oxidation capacity of reagent presented as Cr(VI) > KMnO4 > CH3COOOH > H2O2.

Furthermore, the FL quenched by other oxidizing agents was estimated (Fig. 5e). Only strong oxidizing agents like Cr(VI) and K2MnO4 can substantially quench the FL, while weak oxidizing agents (CH3COOOH, and H2O2) just have a quenching efficiency around 0.17, much lower than 0.9 of Cr(VI). Meanwhile, the FL quenching efficiency is consistent with the decrease of FT-IR spectrum at 835 cm−1 in Fig. 5d. For the character that only strong oxidant destroys the xylan passivation [66], the GQDs show good selectivity and sensitivity in the detection of Cr(VI).

Based on these results mentioned above, the selectivity and sensitivity of the GQDs could be tailored by two strategies for the detection of other substance. Firstly, fluorescence emission/excitation could be tuned to be overlapped with the absorption of analyte by controlling oxidation degree [67], heteroatom doping [68], surface functionalization [69], size regulating [70]. Subsequently, the induced inner-filter effect would quench the fluorescence of GQDs and realize the detection of the analyte. Secondly, due to the affinity between –OH and Fe3+ [32], –COO- and Hg2+ [71], B element and electron rich groups [72], Glutathione and Mn2+ [73], et al, it is an effective way to modify GQDs with specific functional group to detect preferred substance. And the interaction between them would influence fluorescence properties and thus improves the selectivity and sensitivity of probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cr(VI) detection with CQDs/PAAM hydrogel embedded microfluidic chips through smart phones
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the requirement of expensive instruments like fluorescence spectrophotometer and highly trained personnel, the in-time, on-site and accurate Cr(VI) determination is not accessible. The delay or lack of Cr(VI) monitoring may lead to healthcare issues for its high toxicity and facile absorption by human. Therefore, an affordable and portable instrument was fabricated by integrated CQDs/PAAM hydrogel to a microfluidic chip. Combined with smart phone, this equipment could be promising alternatives for conventional equipment and providing a more convenient method for point-of-care testing.

In order to fabricate a portable on-site detection equipment for Cr(VI), GQDs (0.0244% wt.) were embedded in PAAm hydrogel to improve their portability and sensitivity (the inset of Fig. 6 a). Under the radiation of a UV beam of 365 nm, the FL hydrogel emits bright blue light, the same as GQDs aqueous solution (the inset of Fig. 6a). Particularly, CNF was added to strengthen the mechanical properties of the FL hydrogel for its hydrophilicity, high elastic modulus and strength [74]. Although, CNF had intrinsic fluorescence, the FL intensity of GQDs was more than 10 times of CNF in hydrogel, as shown in Fig. S5. Furthermore, there was no difference with the FL of PAAm hydrogel and CNF/PAAm hydrogel. Therefore, the intrinsic fluorescence of CNF would not disturb the detection of Cr(VI). To investigate the mechanical properties, both compressive and tensile tests were used (Fig. 6a, Fig. 6b, Table S1). As shown in Fig. 6a, the compressive stress at strain of 100% is increased with dosage of CNF until reached maximum at 0.6%, and then it decreases. The changing tendency of ultimate tensile strength is the same with compressive stress (Fig. 6b). But the average elastic modulus always increases with the increase of CNF dosage. These results reveal that the mechanical properties are strengthen by CNF, and the sample with 0.6% CNF dosage is chosen for further application. The micromorphology of the CNF strengthened hydrogel was measured by SEM. As shown in Fig. 6c, CNF is distributed on the surface of hydrogel. The internal structure of the hydrogel is porous (Fig. 6d), which is benificial for in efficient binding or capture of Cr(VI).

The FL hydrogel was prepared in “Y” shape to convey that “Yes, there is no problem in the water”. By assembling the FL hydrogel in microfluidic chip, a convenient device for detection of Cr(VI) was fabricated (Fig. 1). The concentration of Cr(VI) can be evaluated through analyzing the decrease of brightness after injecting water containing Cr(VI). As shown in Fig. 7 a, after injecting 3 μM Cr(VI) aqueous solution to microfluidic chip, the brightness of the FL hydrogel quickly falls from 100% to 71.52% in 9 s. When the injecting time prolonged longer than 9 s, the decrease of brightness becomes slow, which was caused by the leaching of GQDs from hydrogel in water. Hence, the decrease of brightness at 9 s was used to represent the FL quenching caused by Cr(VI).

As shown in Fig. 7b, in the range from 3 μM to 75 μM, the relationship between decrease of brightness and concentration of Cr(VI) can be described with a function of G9s/G0 = 7.91–0.1864c(Cr(VI)) (μM) (R2 = 0.9988). Calculated as 3 times standard deviation and slope, the limit of detection is 0.10 μM, which is lower than the detection result by GQDs solution (0.43 μM). By using Stern-Volmer constant Ksv and relative standard deviation (RSD) of detecting results to represent the sensitivity and reproducibility, respectively, the comparison with other reported methods was made and shown in Table S2. Compared with other fluorescent probes, this detection method had higher sensitivity, lower LOD and comparable reproducibility, showing promising potential for the detection of Cr(VI).

To investigate the reliability of the fluorescence sensing method in practical application, lake water was used as real polluted water samples and Cr(VI) was spiked in the lake water, following detection by the proposed method. The mechanism of this visual detection was based on the comparison of FL decrease by test samples with standard curve (Fig. 7b). A smart phone was used to investigate the FL decrease, which could capture the image of FL hydrogel and analysis the grayscale. And with the aid of image processing apps, the brightness of the image was digitized as grayscale, which was equal to fluorescence intensity. Therefore, by analyzing the change of brightness after addition of Cr(VI), the concentration of Cr(VI) could be determined. The FL of the hydrogel was quenched with the diffusion of Cr(VI) into the hydrogel matrix, and thus the fluorescence response was slower than GQDs aqueous solution (Fig. 7a) but was faster than that of reported research [75]. As shown in Table 3 , the obtained recovery rates were in the rate of 97%∼104%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was below 4.6% (n = 3), which were comparable with that measured by ICP-OES. These results confirmed that the detection of Cr(VI) by CQDs/PAAM hydrogel in a microfluidic chips through smart phones was feasible in actual water.

N-doped single-layered GQDs was facilely prepared from xylan in NaOH/urea through hydrothermal reaction, providing a new strategy to fabricate single-layered GQDs by nonaromatic molecules. For the passivation layer of incompletely carbonized xylan, the GQDs showed good selectivity and sensitivity in the detection of Cr(VI). GQDs were embedded in PAAm hydrogel to fabricate FL hydrogel. And a portable and cost-effective Cr(VI) detection equipment was constructed by integration of the FL hydrogel in a microfluidic chip, which had comparable accuracy with ICP-OES. The equipment could be scaled up by the miniaturization and integration of UV lamp and syringe pump, and the attachment of other modules for pretreatment, extraction or concentration. This research laid a good foundation for multiple detection of ions and other containments in water by a microfluidic chip in the future.
